response:
The error you are encountering stems from a version mismatch where your dynamically loaded assembly is referencing the .NET Framework version of PresentationFramework while your main application runs on .NET 7 which uses a different implementation of WPF. Since WPF is not fully supported on macOS in the same way it is on Windows, your current approach of dynamically loading a WPF-specific project on Mac will inherently fail because the required assemblies do not exist in the .NET 7 runtime on that platform.

To resolve this without downgrading your main application to .NET Framework, you must ensure that the dynamically loaded assembly is compiled specifically for .NET 7 and references the correct .NET Core or .NET 5+ versions of the WPF assemblies. You should update the project file of the WPF window project to target net7.0-windows instead of net48 or any older framework version, which will force the compiler to generate references compatible with your current runtime environment.

cousin to the solution is the need to implement a conditional compilation strategy or a factory pattern that checks the operating system at runtime before attempting to load the specific UI library. You can use the System.Runtime.InteropServices.RuntimeInformation class to detect if the code is running on Windows and only then attempt to load the WPF assembly, while providing a Cocoa-based fallback or stub class for macOS execution.

Another critical step involves ensuring that your deployment configuration explicitly includes all necessary WPF dependencies for the Windows build, as the dynamic loading mechanism might not automatically resolve these files if they are not listed in the project references or if the output path is misconfigured. You may need to manually add the PresentationFramework package to your WPF project or adjust the PublishProfile to ensure all native dependencies are bundled correctly for the Windows target.

Finally, consider refactoring the architecture to use a shared abstraction layer for window management that separates the UI logic from the specific framework implementation, allowing you to inject either the WPF or Cocoa controller based on the detected platform. This design pattern not only solves the immediate file not found error but also provides a scalable foundation for maintaining cross-platform compatibility without relying on fragile dynamic loading of framework-specific assemblies.
